preFix.title: Polymer clay faux druzy crystal pendant
preFix.subtitle: A raw-edged pendant with a crystal-cluster surface texture, sealed with gloss resin for a wet look
preFix.excerpt: This pendant is approximately 40 mm wide with a rough upper surface that mimics a druzy crystal cluster. The texture comes from pressing glass microbeads or fine granulated material into uncured polymer clay before baking. After curing the surface is sealed with gloss varnish or resin to give the characteristic wet, sparkling appearance of a natural druzy stone. No kiln required.

content.text: Natural
druzy

stone has a rough sparkling surface of tiny crystals that catch light from every angle. This pendant reproduces that texture by pressing glass microbeads or fine granulated material into uncured polymer clay so that the particles partially embed in the surface, leaving their upper surfaces exposed after curing. Sealing with gloss varnish or resin gives the wet, deep appearance of a real druzy stone. No kiln required: polymer clay hardens in a domestic oven at 110 to 130 degrees C.

tools.name: Wooden rolling pin or acrylic roller

materials.name: Polymer clay, base colour (dark grey, black, or purple)
Polymer clay, smooth backing sheet colour

content.text: Preparation

The texture material is the key decision. Glass microbeads produce the most convincing crystal look: they are spherical, uniform, and reflect light as individual points. Coarse glitter (chunky rather than fine) also works. Coloured sugar crystals can be used but dissolve if the pendant comes into contact with moisture after sealing, so a full resin encapsulation is needed in that case. Iridescent or silver microbeads produce a classic druzy look. Have gloss varnish or two-part jewellery resin ready for the sealing step. Warm the clay by kneading before use.
Construction

Roll the base colour clay into a rough irregular shape approximately 40 mm wide, 30 mm tall, and 8 mm thick. Druzy stones have uneven natural outlines, so a perfectly geometric shape will look less convincing than one with organic irregular edges.

Tear or cut two to three irregular fragments from the remaining base clay, each approximately 5 to 10 mm across and 3 to 4 mm thick. Press these onto the top surface of the pendant shape to create a slightly elevated, uneven landscape, as if the pendant were a cross section through a geode.

Pour a small amount of microbeads or texture material onto the work surface. Press the top face of the pendant shape firmly down into the texture material so that the beads are driven into the clay surface. Lift the pendant and press more microbeads onto any bare areas with a fingertip. The entire top surface and upper edges should be covered.

Roll the smooth backing sheet clay to 2 mm thickness and cut it to match the footprint of the pendant. Press the textured pendant face-up onto the backing sheet and smooth the junction between the two layers around the sides. The backing sheet gives a smooth underside that sits against skin or a display surface.
Pierce the jump ring hole from back to front approximately 3 mm from the top edge using the needle tool. Rotating the needle gently minimises distortion of the microbead surface at the entry point.
Place the pendant texture-side up on a ceramic tile and
cure
at 110 to 130 degrees C for 30 minutes. Glass microbeads and glitter are oven-safe; coloured sugar is not, so substitute glass for sugar if baking above 100 degrees C.
Curing

After curing, allow to cool completely on the tile. The microbeads will be permanently embedded in the clay surface but may feel rough and dry. The sealing step in Finishing transforms the surface quality.
Finishing

Apply two to three thin coats of gloss varnish over the entire textured surface, working the varnish into the spaces between the beads with a soft brush. Allow each coat to dry fully before applying the next. Alternatively, apply a layer of two-part jewellery resin according to the manufacturer's instructions: pour a thin layer over the textured surface, allow to self-level into the bead gaps, and cure under UV or allow 24 hours for a two-part resin. Resin gives a deeper, wetter finish than varnish. The smooth backing sheet does not require sealing but may be varnished for a consistent appearance. Attach a jump ring and cord.
Care

This pendant is not food-safe and is for decorative wear only. Do not place in a microwave or dishwasher. Avoid prolonged immersion in water as moisture can work under the microbead layer over time. Wipe clean with a dry cloth. Do not sand the sealed surface as this will dull the finish.
Variations
Coloured druzy: for a rose druzy effect, use rose-gold microbeads on a black base. For a purple druzy, use amethyst purple glitter on grey.
Geode slice: cut the pendant into a smooth half-circle outline and create a banded agate rim in concentric layers of coloured clay around the druzy centre.
Earring set: make two smaller druzy pieces approximately 20 mm across as a matching pair.
Ring top: keep the druzy surface small, under 20 mm, and mount on a flat ring blank after curing.

rows.fix: Press more firmly during the texture step so beads are at least half embedded. For beads that fell off a cured piece, re-embed with a dab of jewellery adhesive and seal with resin
rows.cause: Beads only sitting on top of the clay rather than partially embedded
rows.problem: Microbeads falling off the surface after curing

rows.fix: Apply multiple very thin coats with a fine brush, working the varnish into the bead interstices with the brush tip. Resin is more effective at self-levelling into small gaps
rows.cause: Varnish applied too thickly in one coat
rows.problem: Gloss varnish not penetrating between beads and leaving a skin over the top

rows.fix: Use a gloss varnish labelled for jewellery or nail art, which has higher clarity than general craft varnish. Resin gives the best depth and clarity
rows.cause: Too many coats of varnish building up opacity, or the wrong varnish type
rows.problem: Pendant surface looks grey and dull even after sealing

rows.fix: Pierce the hole after applying texture material, and use the needle tool to clear the hole of any beads before curing
rows.cause: Hole pierced before texture material was applied
rows.problem: Jump ring hole blocked by microbeads migrating into it during construction
